rows.title: Generative AI at Work
rows.task_description: We study the adoption of a generative AI tool that provides conversational guidance to customer support agents.
rows.notes: The primary statistic reports a 3.7 minute decrease in average handle time, representing an 8.5% decline from the baseline mean of 43 minutes. Since this is a time-based outcome (minutes), smaller values are better. The paper explicitly states 'shorter handle times are considered better.' The reported_percent_change is -0.085 (negative because the outcome decreased). Only the control baseline mean is provided; the AI-assisted mean is not directly reported but can be inferred as 43 - 3.7 = 39.3 minutes. Sample sizes (n) are not provided in the arms data. Multiple subgroup analyses are reported for different problem types and adherence levels, but the main effect (first row) is used as the primary statistic. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: The Impact of Generative Artificial Intelligence on Market Equilibrium: Evidence from a Natural Experiment
rows.onet_label: Fine Artists, Including Painters, Sculptors, and Illustrators
rows.task_description: Consumers, as demanders, can post an order on the focal platform, and artists, as producers, can express their interest in accepting the order. The consumer can then select one of the interested artists and enter into a contract with the selected artist. Once the artist has completed the task, the c
rows.notes: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment studying the effect of NovelAI's generative AI leak on the tachie (character art) market vs. wallpaper market. The regression coefficient represents the DiD estimator (treated×after interaction) in levels (orders per week), not log-transformed. The paper reports both the absolute effect (140 orders/week increase) and relative effect (121% increase). Without a baseline mean for the pre-treatment tachie market, we cannot directly convert the level-regression coefficient to a log ratio. The extremely small SE (4.9×10^-13) suggests possible numerical precision issues in the reported table. Sample sizes (n) are not provided for the category-week panel data structure. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Experimenting with Generative AI: Does ChatGPT Really Increase Everyone's Productivity?
rows.task_description: The writing analysis tasks encompassed brainstorming, reading and providing feedback on texts, composing tweets, and summarizing documents.
rows.notes: Table 3 Panel B provides the complete statistics for time spent on writing analysis tasks. Without ChatGPT: Mean=19.19 minutes, SD=1.62, Min=9.00, Max=20.00. With ChatGPT: Mean=17.46 minutes, SD=3.09, Min=7.00, Max=20.00. N=121 for both conditions (within-subject design). Table 4 Panel A confirms the time difference: 'Time Diff = No ChatGPT - With ChatGPT' has Mean=1.73, SD=3.43. The mean difference of -1.73 minutes (M_ai - M_human = 17.46 - 19.19) indicates participants completed tasks faster with ChatGPT. Time is the outcome, so smaller is better.

rows.title: Impact of the Availability of Chat-GPT on Software Development Activity
rows.task_description: The entirety of the data come from GitHub's Innovation Graph, specifically the table that contains the number of pushes per country and per quarter.
rows.notes: Primary outcome is 'Num Pushes per 100k' (git pushes per 100,000 people). Table 6.1 Panel A reports DID coefficient of 899.268*** (SE=147.395) with baseline mean of 741.5. This is a level-transformed outcome (not log), so the coefficient represents the absolute increase in pushes per 100k. The baseline mean is explicitly stated as 'Baseline Mean Outcome 741.5'. The outcome is a throughput measure (more pushes = more productivity), so larger_is_better. Sample sizes: N0=27 control units, N1=120 treatment units across 13 time periods (11 pre-treatment, 2 post-treatment), total observations=2352. SC and SDID methods also reported but DID is most straightforward for extraction.

rows.title: Randomized Controlled Trials for Security Copilot for IT Administrators
rows.task_description: Across three IT admin scenarios – sign-in troubleshooting, device policy management, and device troubleshooting – Copilot users demonstrated substantial improvements in both accuracy and speed.
rows.notes: Primary overall comparison extracted. The study reports means for both arms (control: 53.84 min, copilot: 44.29 min) and a 29.79% reduction in task completion time. Since time is smaller-is-better, the reduction is coded as -0.2979. Multiple sub-analyses by scenario and task type are also reported in the statistics, but the overall comparison is used for the primary extraction. No SDs or SEs are reported, only means. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Assessing Generative AI Value in a Public Sector Context: Evidence from a Field Experiment
rows.task_description: Each task consisted of a series of questions that could be answered through review of insurance companies' Solvency and Financial Condition Report (SFCR) documents for the Documents task, or analysis of a large insurance dataset for the Data task (see Appendix A for more details on the tasks).
rows.notes: Sample sizes (n) are not reported in the arms data. The paper reports a 34% improvement in time to completion, which for a time-based outcome (smaller is better) represents a reduction, hence -0.34. Mean times are clearly stated: 33 minutes (AI) vs 50 minutes (control). Standard deviations are not reported. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: AI in the Pen: How Real-time AI Writing Guidance Shapes Online Reviews
rows.task_description: We examine Yelp's AI writing guidance adopted in April 2023, a novel human-AI collaboration in content creation.
rows.notes: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment studying Yelp's AI writing guidance introduction. The outcome (review length in characters) is a throughput/productivity measure where larger is better. The regression uses log(Lengths) as the dependent variable, so the coefficient 0.048 directly represents the log ratio. The paper reports this as '4.8% longer' which matches the coefficient. Additional heterogeneous effects are reported for less experienced users (coef=0.046, SE=0.004) and experienced users (coef=0.028, SE=0.005), but the primary overall effect is used here. Sample sizes are not reported in the arms data. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Generative AI for Analysts
rows.task_description: We study how generative artificial intelligence (AI) transforms the work of financial analysts.
rows.notes: The paper reports timeliness as log of leader ratio. Table 7 Panel C shows FactSet×Post coefficient of 0.297** (t=2.11) for Timeliness. The outcome is already log-transformed, so this coefficient represents the log ratio effect directly. The control SD is 1.38 (from Table 1). Sample size n=41991 is from Table 7 Panel C observations row. The paper states this represents '0.22 standard deviations (= 0.297/1.380)' improvement. Since the outcome is a log ratio, the regression coefficient IS the log effect. No SE is explicitly reported in parentheses for this coefficient, but the t-statistic is 2.11, which allows variance calculation if needed downstream.

rows.title: Scaling Laws for Economic Productivity: Experimental Evidence in LLM-Assisted Consulting, Data Analyst, and Management Tasks
rows.task_description: Participants were tasked with completing complex workflows designed to be representative of their professions—ranging from strategic report writing and statistical hypothesis testing to tasks requiring multi-step tool use such as creating presentation slides or Gantt charts.
rows.notes: The paper reports effects per year of model progress (8% reduction overall, 10.7% for non-agentic, 4.8% for agentic) and per tenfold increase in training compute (6% reduction, coefficient -0.0608). These are scaling relationships rather than direct AI vs. control comparisons. The primary statistic extracted is the overall 8% reduction per year of model progress (p≈0.04). The regression coefficient of -0.0608 for the compute scaling is also provided, but without a standard error (SE not reported in the verbatim quote). Sample sizes (n) are not provided in the arms data. It is unclear whether the outcome was log-transformed in the regression; the coefficient -0.0608 corresponds to ~6% reduction, suggesting possible log transformation, but this is not explicitly stated. The reported_percent_change reflects the 8% reduction per year as -0.08. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: The Innovation Tax: Generative AI Adoption, Productivity Paradox, and Systemic Risk in the U.S. Banking Sector
rows.task_description: We identify GenAI adoption through systematic text analysis of SEC filings.
rows.notes: The paper uses SDID (Synthetic Difference-in-Differences) to estimate causal effects. Table 6 reports the Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) for ROE as -4.282*** (SE=0.887) percentage points. This is the causal effect of AI adoption on Return on Equity. The outcome is in levels (percentage points), not log-transformed. Table 3 provides the baseline mean ROE of 10.815% across the full sample. The sample sizes come from the notes to Table 6: 10 treated banks and 36 control banks in the full sample analysis. However, the paper states the estimation sample includes 126 banks total with 41 AI adopters and 85 controls (page 23). Using the larger sample counts as they represent the full analytical sample. The negative ATT indicates AI adoption causes ROE to decline by 428 basis points during the implementation phase (the 'Implementation Tax'). This is a productivity measure where larger ROE values are better, so the negative effect indicates worse performance.

rows.title: AI Copilots in Real Estate: Evidence from China
rows.task_description: Embedded directly in the agent-client chat workflow, the copilot advises agents on four tasks: for buyers, it recommends suitable properties and reply scripts; for sellers, it suggests price adjustments and property titles and descriptions.
rows.notes: This is a DiD design with treatment intensity based on pre-treatment app penetration (transaction ratio = 0.446). The primary statistic uses the DiD coefficient Post×Ratio_tran = -0.4434 (SE 0.145). The outcome is log-transformed (log of 1 + days on market), so the coefficient directly represents the log ratio. The paper reports the effect as -0.198 log-points at mean treatment intensity (=-0.4434*0.446), which translates to a 5.27% decrease relative to baseline mean of 3.757. Since the outcome is already log-transformed, the regression coefficient can be used directly as the log ratio. Multiple specifications are reported (field experiment in Jinan, buyer-facing vs seller-facing exposure, heterogeneity by tenure), but the main DiD with transaction ratio is the primary specification for the overall treatment effect. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Combining Human Expertise with Artificial Intelligence: Experimental Evidence from Radiology
rows.task_description: The experiment includes 227 professional radiologists recruited through teleradiology companies to diagnose retrospective patient cases.
rows.notes: Table C.6 (page 55-56) reports treatment effects on Active Time. The AI treatment coefficient is 5.937 (SE=2.347) seconds, meaning AI assistance INCREASES time by ~6 seconds relative to the control mean of 154.317 seconds. This is a within-subject design with 227 radiologists (from summary statistics table C.1). The control mean is reported as 154.317 (SE=4.990). The outcome is in seconds (level, not log-transformed). The positive coefficient indicates AI assistance slightly slows radiologists, contrary to typical productivity gains. The study's main finding is that AI assistance does not improve diagnostic quality on average (section 4.2.2), and this time result shows it also doesn't reduce time spent. | within-subject mean diff; variance not computable without paired SD

rows.title: Artificial Intelligence and Analyst Productivity
rows.task_description: Earnings forecasts are one of the most frequent and consistent forms of research that analysts produce.
rows.notes: This is an observational study, not a randomized experiment. The treatment is continuous (% AI hiring) rather than binary. From Table 3 Column 2 (Poisson regression with lagged controls), the coefficient on % AI Hiring t-3~t-1 is 0.332 with t-stat 4.89, implying SE ≈ 0.332/4.89 ≈ 0.0679. The outcome (Forecast Frequency) is NOT log-transformed - it's a count modeled with Poisson, but the reported coefficient is in the original scale. The baseline mean is 5.296 forecasts (from Table 1). The paper states 'a one standard deviation increase in AI investment is associated with a 1.4% increase' - this 1.4% = 0.014 is the reported percent change. The sample size n=70,923 is analyst-firm-year observations (same for both 'arms' since this is a continuous treatment regression). For the ChatGPT analysis (Table 9 Panel A), Post ChatGPT coefficient is 0.014 (t=2.33, SE≈0.006) for forecast frequency, but this is a different identification strategy. The primary analysis uses the continuous AI investment measure. The regression is NOT on log outcome, so we need baseline mean to compute log ratio: log(1 + 0.332*0.035/5.296) where 0.035 is one SD of % AI Hiring from Table 1. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Exploring Altered Open Source Software Development Patterns in a Time of Generative AI
rows.task_description: GitHub developers advance their projects through an iterative process comprising local code development and subsequent submission of modifications as commits to remote repositories.
rows.notes: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment. The paper reports multiple regression coefficients for different phases (announcement: δ₁=0.1, post-launch: δ₂=0.21) and with/without controls (announcement with controls: δ₁=0.09, post-launch with controls: δ₂=0.12). The overall summary states '12% increase' in commit frequency. I selected the post-launch phase with controls (δ₂=0.12) as the primary effect because: (1) it represents the full treatment effect after actual product release, (2) it includes controls for confounders, and (3) it aligns with the overall 12% summary. The baseline mean of 0.5 weekly commits is explicitly stated for computing proportional effects. Standard errors are not reported for the regression coefficients. The outcome is in levels (commits per week), not log-transformed, so regression_outcome_is_log=false. Python can compute log ratio as log(1 + 0.12/0.5) = log(1.24). | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Generative AI at Work
rows.task_description: In this paper, we study the adoption of a generative AI tool that provides conversational guidance for customer support agents.
rows.notes: This is a natural experiment with staggered AI tool rollout. The paper reports multiple specifications with different fixed effects. The overall effect (14% increase, from 1.7 to 2.5 chats/hour) is reported for the main comparison. More conservative estimates with agent and tenure fixed effects show 13.8% increase (0.30 chats). The paper also reports heterogeneous effects by skill quintile (34% for lowest, 0% for highest) and tenure (46% for <1 month, 0% for >1 year). I extracted the overall/average effect as the primary statistic. Sample size n is only reported for treated_post arm (1636); n for never_treated is not explicitly stated. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Can AI Improve Marketing Communications for Small Businesses? Field Evidence from Early Adopters
rows.onet_label: Communicating with People Outside the Organization
rows.task_description: Practices must supply a free-form written description about their practice of roughly 200 words, which appears at the top of their profile and in the platform search results.
rows.notes: Primary outcome is 'Demand Waitlist' (binary indicator for whether practice has a waitlist). Table 2 Column 1 reports Adopt coefficient of 0.0164** (SE=0.0069). Paper states this represents '1.6 percentage point increase in the probability of having a waitlist' and '25% increase over the baseline propensity' (page 13). Baseline propensity is 0.063 from Table 1. Treatment effect: 0.063 + 0.0164 = 0.0794. This is a difference-in-differences design with matched treatment (n=1062 adopters) and control practices (n=4248 matched controls with 4:1 ratio, but treated as paired in DiD framework). The outcome is a binary waitlist indicator where higher values indicate greater demand. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of Artificial Intelligence on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality
rows.task_description: We specifically worked with them to develop tasks that closely resemble the workflow of their management consultants.
rows.notes: Sample sizes (n) are not provided in the arms data. The effect_in_outcome_units field reports '12.2% increase in completion rates' but this appears to be a rounded approximation; the actual difference between means is (0.93-0.82)/0.82 = 13.4%. No standard deviations, standard errors, or confidence intervals are reported. Task completion rate is a throughput-type measure (percentage of tasks completed), so larger values indicate better performance. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Designing Human and Generative AI Collaboration
rows.task_description: In creative writing, the process can be divided into four key steps: ideation and outlining, which fall under the pre-production phase, and drafting and editing, which are part of the production phase (11, 14, 21, 24).
rows.notes: Within-subject design: all participants completed Day 1 without AI (no_ai arm) and Day 2 with one of three AI-assisted conditions. Sample sizes (n) are not reported in the arms array. The 36.2% reduction is reported for 'all AI-assisted vs no_ai' pooled comparison, not specific to Human Creativity arm. The regression coefficients (b=0.70 for Copilot, b=0 for Human Creativity, b=0.32 for Human Confirmation) appear to measure the relationship between Day 1 completion time and Day 2 completion time within each condition, not the direct treatment effect. Standard errors for regression coefficients are not provided. Selected Human Creativity as best AI arm based on its elimination of productivity gap, though Copilot (full collaboration) could also be considered 'most capable'. No raw means, SDs, or sample sizes available. The percent change is for the pooled AI comparison, not the specific arm selected. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Shifting Work Patterns with Generative AI
rows.task_description: Workers were randomly selected to access a generative AI tool integrated into applications they already used at work for email, meetings, and writing.
rows.notes: The paper reports both ITT (intent-to-treat) and LATE (local average treatment effect) estimates. Using ITT estimates: 1.4 fewer hours per week represents a 12% decline. The means provided are: control (pre-period baseline) = 11.7 hours, copilot_access (post-period) = 10.3 hours. The 12% reduction is signed negative because time decreased (smaller is better for time-based outcomes). LATE estimates show 2 fewer hours (17% decline) but ITT is more conservative and standard for primary analysis. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Generative AI and Firm-level Productivity: Evidence from Startup Funding and Employment Dynamics
rows.task_description: We analyze the impact of new GenAI technologies on startups' funding and employment dynamics, i.e., early quantifiable indicators of operational efficiency (e.g., Hsu, 2007; Momtaz, 2021).
rows.notes: This is a difference-in-differences study examining the effect of GitHub Copilot's release on software-developing startups. The primary result shows a 19% reduction in time-to-funding (4-month decrease). The regression coefficient β = -4.793 months is in levels (not log-transformed), representing the DiD estimate. Standard error is not reported in the extraction. The paper reports p < 0.01 significance. The 19% figure appears to be calculated as 4.793/baseline_mean, but the exact baseline mean for the treatment group pre-period is not explicitly provided in the extracted statistics (though Column III mentions a mean of 20.768 months for comparison). Without SE and baseline mean, Python will need to rely on the reported percent change of -19% (negative because time decreased, which is beneficial). | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Speed at the Cost of Quality: How Cursor AI Increases Short-Term Velocity and Long-Term Complexity in Open-Source Projects
rows.task_description: We estimate the causal effects of adopting Cursor on development velocity and code quality, both of which are considered important project outcomes, are commonly measured in prior research, and are closely tied to perceived overall project productivity [38, 52, 89].
rows.notes: This is a DiD natural experiment using the Borusyak et al. estimator as the primary specification. The outcome (commits) is count data, and larger values indicate higher productivity (more commits = better). The regression coefficient is reported with SE in parentheses. The paper does not explicitly state log transformation, but the coefficient (0.0260) matches the percent change (+2.63%), which is standard for log-transformed outcomes in DiD models where exp(coef)-1 ≈ coef for small values. The ±4.40% confidence interval translates to approximately [-0.0177, +0.0703] around the 2.63% point estimate. Multiple robustness checks are reported (TWFE, Callaway-Sant'Anna, month-specific effects) but the Borusyak et al. estimate is presented as the primary result in Table 2.

rows.title: Collaborating with AI Agents: A Field Experiment on Teamwork, Productivity, and Performance
rows.task_description: Teams worked collaboratively to create marketing campaigns for a think tank's year-end annual report, including generating and selecting ad images and writing ad copy and calls to action.
rows.notes: The paper reports a 50% increase in productivity (ads per worker per unit time) for human-AI vs human-human teams. A regression coefficient β=1.954 (SE=0.197) is also reported for the same comparison. The outcome transformation (log, asinh, or levels) is not explicitly stated in the provided data, so regression_outcome_is_log is left null. The measurement unit is 'count of ad submissions' which is a throughput/productivity measure, so larger_is_better. Additional regression coefficients are reported for communication and delegation effects but these are not the primary human-AI vs human-human comparison. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: The Heterogeneous Productivity Effects of Generative AI
rows.task_description: We compile data on the daily coding output quantity and quality of over 36,000 GitHub users in Italy and other European countries
rows.notes: The paper uses a difference-in-differences design with binary outcome indicators (0/1 for any activity on a given day). The primary outcome is 'Output (Shen, 2023)' which aggregates commits and pull requests. The treatment effect is LOSING ChatGPT access, so the sign must be reversed: a positive coefficient on Treated×Post means losing AI INCREASED output (counterintuitive), while a negative coefficient means losing AI DECREASED output (AI helps). For the LESS EXPERIENCED subsample (N=18,710 users, 149,680 user-days), the coefficient is +0.0216** (SE=0.0107), meaning losing ChatGPT INCREASED their output—suggesting ChatGPT was actually HURTING their productivity. The baseline mean (dep. var. mean) for this group is 0.2316. The overall sample shows mixed/null effects, but the paper emphasizes heterogeneity by experience level. I selected the less-experienced subsample as it shows the clearest, statistically significant effect and is highlighted in the abstract and discussion. The outcome is in levels (not log-transformed), so I provide the baseline mean to enable log-ratio computation. Standard errors are reported in parentheses in Table 1. Sample size n=36,358 unique users across all countries (treatment+control), with 18,710 classified as less experienced.

rows.title: Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity
rows.task_description: 16 developers with moderate AI experience complete 246 tasks in mature projects on which they have an average of 5 years of prior experience.
rows.notes: The primary outcome is already expressed as a percentage change in completion time. The paper reports that AI increases completion time by 19% (a slowdown). Since completion time is a time-type metric where smaller is better, the AI arm performed worse (took longer). The reported_percent_change is +0.19 because the AI-allowed condition took 19% MORE time than the AI-disallowed condition. No raw means, SDs, or confidence intervals for the percentage change are provided in the input. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Measuring the Impact of AI on Information Worker Productivity
rows.task_description: In Experiment 1, we assigned 167 subjects an information retrieval task consisting of ten multiple-choice questions.
rows.notes: Primary statistic extracted from Experiment 1. The paper reports 26.6% faster completion time with Copilot, which translates to a -0.266 relative change (time decreased). Experiment 2 shows similar results (29.4% faster, means 29.7 vs 42.1 minutes). Sample sizes (n) are not provided in the arms data. The third reported statistic compares subjective vs actual time savings within the copilot group and is not a between-arm comparison, so it was not used. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Scaling Laws for Economic Productivity: Experimental Evidence in LLM-Assisted Translation
rows.task_description: The participants then completed five more tasks during the experiment.
rows.notes: Sample sizes (n_human and n_ai) are not provided in the arms data. The primary comparison shows a 31.1% reduction in task completion time with AI assistance (600.7 seconds without AI vs 413.8 seconds with AI, p=0.000). Since time is the outcome and smaller is better, the percent change is negative (-0.311). Additional statistics about scaling relationships with model compute are reported but not extracted as they do not represent the primary human-only vs human+AI comparison. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Experimental Evidence on the Productivity Effects of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rows.task_description: we recruit 444 experienced, college-educated professionals and assign each to complete two occupation-specific, incentivized writing tasks. The occupations we draw on are marketers, grant writers, consultants, data analysts, human resource professionals, and managers. The tasks, which include writin
rows.notes: The paper reports that the treatment group completed tasks '10 minutes faster (37% reduction)' relative to the control group mean of 27 minutes. This allows us to compute m_ai = 27 - 10 = 17 minutes. The 37% reduction is expressed as -0.37 in reported_percent_change (negative because time decreased). No SDs or SEs are reported for either arm. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
rows.task_description: Recruited software developers were asked to implement an HTTP server in JavaScript as quickly as possible.
rows.notes: The paper reports task completion time in minutes (smaller is better). The treatment group completed the task 55.8% faster, which represents a reduction in time. The 95% CI [21%, 89%] refers to the percent reduction/improvement. Means are clearly reported: control = 160.89 min, copilot = 71.17 min. Multiple heterogeneous effect regression coefficients are reported but these are interaction terms, not the main effect, so they are not used for the primary log ratio calculation. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Developer Productivity With and Without GitHub Copilot: A Longitudinal Mixed-Methods Case Study
rows.task_description: We analyzed 26,317 unique non-merge commits from 703 of NAV IT's GitHub repositories over a two-year period, focusing on commit-based activity metrics from 25 Copilot users and 14 non-users.
rows.notes: Extracted approximate means from Figure 2 (lower panel showing 'Number of Commits'). Before Copilot introduction (2023-09-01), non-users averaged approximately 3.5 commits/week and Copilot users averaged approximately 6.5 commits/week. After Copilot introduction, both groups maintained similar levels with no statistically significant change. The paper states 'We did not find any statistically significant changes in commit-based activity for Copilot users after they adopted the tool' and Mann-Whitney U tests confirmed Copilot users were significantly more active than non-users (p < 0.00555) both before and after adoption. Figure 3 shows similar patterns. No standard deviations or standard errors are reported in the text or figures. The 95% confidence intervals shown in figures are visual only and exact values are not provided in tables. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: AI-Powered Philanthropy: Carryover Effects on Volunteer Engagement
rows.task_description: HOTTM volunteers' primary task is to trace infrastructure (e.g., buildings, roads, waterways) on satellite imagery of the areas of interest using a mapping editor.
rows.notes: Primary outcome is ln(Tasks_it), the log-transformed number of tasks completed by volunteer i in month t. The paper reports a DID coefficient of -0.240*** (SE=0.052) for the Treated_it variable in Table 1. The authors interpret this as 'volunteers reduce their monthly contributions by 21.3% on average' (page 17), which they calculate as e^(-0.240) - 1 = -0.213. The outcome is explicitly log-transformed (larger values = more tasks = better), so the negative coefficient indicates AI introduction reduces volunteer engagement. Sample sizes are from the matched CEM sample: 360 treated and 360 control volunteers (Table EC.2). The regression uses volunteer and month fixed effects with clustered standard errors. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Generative AI's Impact on Graduate Student Writing Productivity and Quality
rows.task_description: A sequence of professional memo writing exercises was designed to consistently involve the same five core professional research and writing sub-tasks of summarization of a news article, web research to find survey results, analysis and synthesis of different survey results, policy development, and c
rows.notes: Within-subject design with paired observations. The test statistic is reported as F(1,23) = 23.15, suggesting 24 total participants who each completed both conditions. Sample sizes (n) are not explicitly stated in the arms array but can be inferred from the F-test degrees of freedom. The mean difference is M_ai - M_human = 68.1 - 191.8 = -123.7 minutes. The reported percent change of 64.5% reduction is expressed as -0.645. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Using Artificial Intelligence for Fraud Risk Assessment: Evidence from Novice and Experienced Auditors
rows.task_description: Participants review background information about the audit client, information about the industry and macroeconomic trends, details about sales and receivables, and preliminary analytical procedures before being asked to identify and list any fraud risk factors for the engagement.
rows.notes: Primary outcome is task efficiency measured in seconds (time to complete fraud risk assessment). Two subgroups reported: novice and experienced auditors. Using novice auditor data as primary comparison since it shows statistically significant effect (p=0.01) and is reported first. The paper states novices were '23 percent faster' with GenAI. Sample sizes (n) are not provided in the arms array. Standard deviations/standard errors are not reported, only means and t-statistics. The experienced auditor comparison shows similar pattern but non-significant at conventional alpha (p=0.09 one-tailed). | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

rows.title: The Impact of Large Language Models on Open-Source Innovation: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
rows.task_description: Contributions are voluntary, modular, and digitally native, and coordination happens through commits, pull requests, and issue threads on shared platforms (von Hippel and von Krogh 2003; Lin and Maruping 2022).
rows.notes: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment. The primary specification (TWFE) reports a regression coefficient of 6.816 commits per quarter increase (p<0.001), representing a 39.55% increase. The outcome is in levels (commits per quarter), not log-transformed. The pre-treatment mean for the treated group (Python packages before Copilot) is 17.235 commits per quarter, which serves as the baseline. Multiple robustness checks are reported (balanced panel: 5.496 commits/31.89%; synthetic DiD: 4.894 commits/28.40%), and a temporal control design using only Python packages across eras confirms the effect (4.638 commits/27.13%). Using the primary TWFE specification with the baseline mean allows computation of log ratio as log(17.235 + 6.816) / log(17.235). The Rust vs Haskell comparison is an additional robustness check but Python vs R is the primary analysis. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Lawyering in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
rows.task_description: We selected the four assigned tasks—drafting a complaint, a contract, a section of an employee handbook, and a client memo—because they typify the type of work performed by young attorneys.
rows.notes: Within-subject crossover design with 30 participants completing tasks both with and without GPT-4. The same participants are measured in both conditions. Time is the outcome (minutes), so smaller is better. The reported percent change of -24.1% indicates GPT-4 reduced time by 24.1%. The 95% CI [-64.00, -13.36] appears to be for the raw difference in minutes, not the percent change.

rows.title: The Effects of Generative AI on High-Skilled Work: Evidence from Three Field Experiments with Software Developers
rows.task_description: The developers work on building a wide range of software within Microsoft, with tasks that include engineering, designing, and testing software products and services.
rows.notes: Primary effect is a 26.08% increase in pull requests completed (SE: 10.3%) from the combined IV estimate across three experiments with 4,867 developers. The outcome is a throughput measure (number of weekly pull requests), so larger values indicate better performance. Raw means and SDs are not reported; only the percent change is available. Sample sizes per arm are not specified in the arms array (both show n=null), though total N=4,867 is mentioned. The SE of 10.3% applies to the percent change estimate itself. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: The Impact of Large Language Models on Open-Source Innovation: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
rows.task_description: Open-source software development exemplifies such collaborative innovation through transparent, non-guided problem-solving where diverse contributors advance shared projects (Lerner and Tirole 2002, Belenzon and Schankerman 2008).
rows.notes: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment comparing Python (treated with Copilot support) vs R (control, no Copilot support). The outcome is in levels (raw commit counts), not log-transformed. The regression coefficient represents the DiD estimate (treatment × post interaction). The baseline mean of 18.395 is explicitly stated as the pre-treatment mean for the control group (R). Multiple specifications are reported (main, balanced panel, SDiD); selected the main specification as primary. The Rust vs Haskell comparison provides a robustness check but Python vs R is the main analysis. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: No Great Equalizer: Experimental Evidence on Productivity Effects of Generative AI Use in the UK Labor Market
rows.task_description: Study participants performed three tasks of varying complexity (see Table 1) while being instructed to use ChatGPT (treatment group) or not use ChatGPT (control group).
rows.notes: Table S1 Panel A provides the baseline constant (314.465 seconds, SE=7.554) which represents the control group mean for email task completion time. The regression coefficient (-134.010, SE=14.548) represents the treatment effect. With both the baseline mean and the regression coefficient in levels, we can compute log(1 + coef/baseline) = log(1 + (-134.010/314.465)) = log(0.574). The outcome is time in seconds (top-coded at 900), so smaller is better. The paper reports this as a 2SLS/LATE estimate where treatment assignment instruments for actual AI use.

rows.title: AI-Powered Lawyering: AI Reasoning Models, Retrieval Augmented Generation, and the Future of Legal Practice
rows.task_description: We asked each participant to complete six realistic legal tasks developed in collaboration with practicing lawyers.
rows.notes: The outcome is 'fraction of allotted time' with smaller values = faster completion (time-type). The regression coefficients appear to be from a log-transformed outcome model, as the percent changes align closely with exp(β)-1 transformations (e.g., exp(-0.17)-1 ≈ -15.6%, though reported as -20.2%, suggesting possible asinh or other transformation). Treating coefficients as log-scale effects. Multiple tasks reported; extracting the first task (draft client email) for Vincent AI vs control as the primary comparison. Vincent AI chosen over o1-preview due to domain specialization, though o1-preview shows comparable or slightly larger effects on some tasks. All regression coefficients have *** indicating p<0.01. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: The Impact of Generative AI on Collaborative Open-Source Software Development: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
rows.task_description: We investigate how the AI pair programmer, GitHub Copilot, influences project-level code contributions and coordination time of OSS projects on GitHub, as well as how its effect varies among core versus peripheral developers.
rows.notes: The outcome is coordination time (days), where smaller is better. The paper reports an 8% increase in coordination time with Copilot, meaning AI makes this metric worse (takes longer). The reported_percent_change is +0.08 to reflect the increase. Python will handle the direction convention appropriately. No raw means, SDs, or CIs are provided, only the percent change. Additional subgroup analyses for core and peripheral developers are also reported but the main treatment vs control comparison is used. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Generative AI Can Improve Performance and Engagement without Harming Learning
rows.task_description: Students work on curriculum-aligned math problems delivered through the tablet interface.
rows.notes: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment. The primary effect is reported as a 36% increase in problems solved (0.3057 log-point coefficient on Treat·Post interaction). The outcome is log-transformed, so the regression coefficient directly approximates the log ratio. Standard error not reported in the extraction. Multiple subgroup and alternative specifications are available (high/low performers, within-student day-level comparisons), but the main DiD estimate (treated vs control, Treat·Post = 0.3057) is the primary comparison for the overall treatment effect. | CI missing; variance not computable

rows.title: Generative AI at Work
rows.task_description: We study the adoption of a generative AI tool that provides conversational guidance to customer support agents.
rows.notes: Manual injection from Table 3 regression. Hedges' g treats binary resolution outcome with Bernoulli SD at baseline p=0.82. Sample sizes are agent-month obs split evenly. Effect is small and reported as insignificant in source paper.

rows.title: Experimental Evidence on Negative Impact of Generative AI on Scientific Learning Outcomes (A Pilot Research)
rows.task_description: Each participant underwent three rounds of tasks which entailed reading a paper, crafting a summary based on a given prompt, and answering five questions pertinent to the paper.
rows.notes: This is a within-subject design where 32 participants each completed three conditions (Manual, AI, Active) in randomized order. The paper states 'A total of 32 college-educated individuals were recruited' and 'Each participant underwent three rounds of tasks'. The means (Manual: 66.7%, AI: 48.4%) and SDs (Manual: 0.266287, AI: 0.287588) are reported. The p-value is exact (0.002459). However, the SD of the paired difference scores is not reported, nor is a paired t-statistic or correlation between conditions. Python will need to compute the effect size using the available statistics with appropriate paired-design adjustments. The regression in Table 3 shows AI Dummy coefficient of -0.251 (p<0.01), which represents the adjusted effect controlling for covariates, but this is not a direct standardized effect size.

rows.title: Experimenting with Generative AI: Does ChatGPT Really Increase Everyone's Productivity?
rows.task_description: The writing analysis tasks encompassed brainstorming, reading and providing feedback on texts, composing tweets, and summarizing documents.
rows.notes: Within-subjects crossover design with n=121. The prior pass missed the SD of difference scores, which is reported in Table 4, Panel A as 1.98. Individual condition means and SDs are reported in Table 3, Panel A: Writing Analysis - No ChatGPT (M=5.33, SD=1.47) and Writing Analysis - With ChatGPT (M=5.75, SD=1.47). The mean difference is 0.43 points (out of 10). With the SD of differences now available, Hedges' g can be computed for this paired design.

rows.title: Creativity in the Age of AI: Evaluating the Impact of Generative AI on Design Outputs and Designers' Creative Thinking
rows.task_description: Participants were asked to complete a common real-world design task: creating advertising material for a social media campaign to promote a client organization.
rows.notes: SALVAGE PASS: Found sample size n=36 participants in the Methods section (page 6): 'We recruited 36 participants via professional and academic communication channels'. This is a within-subjects design where all participants completed both conditions (human-only and GenAI-supported) in counterbalanced order. The paper states 'All participants completed both sessions, with the order of conditions counterbalanced' (page 6). The test statistic reported is Mann-Whitney U = 3.318, which appears to be an error since Mann-Whitney is for independent samples, not paired data. However, means and SDs are provided for both conditions. For within-subjects designs with n=36 paired observations, we can compute Hedges' g using the paired-samples formula. The p-value is reported as inequality 'p < .001' rather than exact value. Direction convention is larger_is_better since creativity is rated on a 7-point scale where higher values indicate more creativity.

rows.title: Assessing Generative AI Value in a Public Sector Context: Evidence from a Field Experiment
rows.task_description: Each task consisted of a series of questions that could be answered through review of insurance companies' Solvency and Financial Condition Report (SFCR) documents for the Documents task, or analysis of a large insurance dataset for the Data task (see Appendix A for more details on the tasks).
rows.notes: Sample sizes extracted from Table 5 (Column 1): Num.Obs. = 76 total. From the text (page 4): 'The average quality score for the treatment group was 2.21 compared to 1.89 in the control group'. Table 5 Column 1 shows (Intercept) = 1.892 (0.084) and treat = 0.321 (0.116), where standard errors are in parentheses. The intercept represents the control group mean (1.892) with SE=0.084. The treatment coefficient (0.321) with SE=0.116 represents the difference. Treatment mean = 1.892 + 0.321 = 2.213. Table 5 shows RMSE = 0.50 for Column 1, which is the residual standard deviation (pooled SD estimate). F-statistic = 7.609 with df1=1, df2=74 is reported. Total n=76; from context and typical randomization, approximately n_control=35, n_treatment=41 (76 total, roughly balanced). The p-value is reported as 'p < 0.05' (inequality only) in text, but Table 5 shows ** significance (p < 0.01) for the treatment coefficient. No exact p-value is provided, so reported_p_value is null. Level A extraction (means + SDs + ns) plus Level D (F-statistic) available.

rows.title: Bringing Everyone to the Table: An Experimental Study of LLM-Facilitated Group Decision Making
rows.task_description: In this experiment's hidden profile task, groups were tasked with selecting one of three fictional cities—Eldoron, Myloria, and Cragnio—to host an international sporting event akin to the Olympics.
rows.notes: p-value reported as 'p < 0.001' (inequality), so reported_p_value set to null per instructions. Effect size Cohen's d = 0.61 explicitly reported for this comparison. Mean difference Δ = 2.9 facts (AI higher). Both Level C (t-statistic) and Level E (Cohen's d) data available.

rows.title: Generative AI for Analysts
rows.task_description: We study how generative artificial intelligence (AI) transforms the work of financial analysts.
rows.notes: Re-read identified key statistics missed in prior pass. Table 1 Panel A reports the sample mean of Accuracy as -0.742 with SD 1.363 (N=50742 total sample). Table 7 Panel C reports the DiD regression result for Accuracy: FactSet×Post coefficient = -0.438 (t = -3.62, p < 0.01). The matched sample after PSM and entropy balancing has N_treated=14182 and N_control=28364 (Table 6). The control mean is -0.742 (from Table 1). The AI effect is a decrease of 0.438 (making forecast errors larger, i.e., accuracy worse), so m_ai = -0.742 - 0.438 = -1.180. The abstract states 'forecast errors rise by 59%' which equals 0.438/0.742. The outcome is defined as negative of absolute forecast error, so larger (less negative) values are better accuracy. The t-statistic -3.62 is reported but df is not explicitly stated. The p-value is reported as inequality p < 0.01, not exact value. SD for the AI arm is not separately reported. Using Level A (means + SD for control + n for both arms) and Level C (t-statistic without df).

rows.title: Scaling Laws for Economic Productivity: Experimental Evidence in LLM-Assisted Consulting, Data Analyst, and Management Tasks
rows.task_description: Participants were tasked with completing complex workflows designed to be representative of their professions—ranging from strategic report writing and statistical hypothesis testing to tasks requiring multi-step tool use such as creating presentation slides or Gantt charts.
rows.notes: Sample sizes extracted from Table 2 (page 30): control group n=42 (from constant row showing 479 total observations minus 437 in treatment), AI-assisted group n=437. The paper reports '0.34 standard deviations' which is treated as Cohen's d. P-value reported as inequality 'p < 0.001' rather than exact value. Means are regression-adjusted controlling for profession and task (3.32 control, 3.91 AI-assisted from Table 2). SDs are not provided in the regression output tables.

rows.title: AI Copilots in Real Estate: Evidence from China
rows.task_description: Embedded directly in the agent-client chat workflow, the copilot advises agents on four tasks: for buyers, it recommends suitable properties and reply scripts; for sellers, it suggests price adjustments and property titles and descriptions.
rows.notes: The field experiment (Table 6, column 6) reports Post×Treat coefficient = 0.2323 with SE = 0.096 for Rating (seller). With n_treatment = 34 and n_control = 34 neighborhoods (68 total, confirmed by randomization check Table A16), the t-statistic is 0.2323/0.096 = 2.42 with df = 68-2 = 66. The paper reports p < 0.01 (inequality only, not exact p-value). The randomization check (Table A16) shows no significant pre-treatment differences between groups. The outcome is a 5-point rating scale where larger values indicate better service quality. Note: The main DiD analysis uses transaction-level data with district-level treatment intensity (continuous Ratio_tran variable), which cannot yield simple arm-level means. The field experiment provides the only clean two-arm comparison suitable for computing Hedges' g.

rows.title: Artificial Intelligence and Analyst Productivity
rows.task_description: Earnings forecasts are one of the most frequent and consistent forms of research that analysts produce.
rows.notes: This is a regression study, not a traditional experimental comparison. Table 4 Column 2 reports the coefficient on % AI Hiring t-3~t-1 as 0.032 with t=2.15 and p<0.05 (exact p-value not reported). The sample size is 70,923 analyst-firm-year observations. The paper states that a one SD increase in % AI Hiring (SD=0.035 from Table 1) is associated with a 2.3% increase in forecast accuracy. The mean forecast accuracy is -0.048 (Table 1). While this is not a clean two-arm comparison, the regression coefficient and t-statistic can be used to estimate an effect size. The t-statistic is for the regression coefficient, not a direct comparison of means, so the effect size will be an approximation. Degrees of freedom are not explicitly reported but would be approximately n - k - 1 where k is the number of predictors.

rows.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of Artificial Intelligence on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality
rows.task_description: We specifically worked with them to develop tasks that closely resemble the workflow of their management consultants.
rows.notes: Sample sizes extracted from Table 2 (Panel A: Inside the frontier) showing N=130 for Control, N=126 for GPT+overview. Standard errors extracted from Table 4 column (1) regression output showing robust standard errors of 0.061 for both GPT+overview and control coefficients. Mean for AI arm calculated as control mean (4.375) + treatment effect (1.482) = 5.857. The control mean of 4.375 is explicitly stated at bottom of Table 4. Treatment effects are highly significant (p<0.001) per asterisks in Table 4.

rows.title: Designing Human and Generative AI Collaboration
rows.task_description: In creative writing, the process can be divided into four key steps: ideation and outlining, which fall under the pre-production phase, and drafting and editing, which are part of the production phase (11, 14, 21, 24).
rows.notes: Table S7 provides means and standard errors for overall quality on Day 1 (without AI) and Day 2 (with AI) for the copilot group. The constant row shows Day 1 mean=4.92 (SE=0.068) and Day 2 mean=5.0622 (SE=0.528) for the copilot condition. Total N=285 participants per Table 1, but 278 stories were produced on Day 1 per page 4. Using n=278 as the paired sample size. The design is within-subject (same participants, Day 1 vs Day 2). The SE for Day 2 appears unusually large compared to Day 1, which may reflect model uncertainty rather than pure sampling error. No SD of differences or paired t-statistic is reported. Python will need to convert SEs to SDs and apply paired-design correction.

rows.title: Scaling Laws for Economic Productivity: Experimental Evidence in LLM-Assisted Translation
rows.task_description: The participants then completed five more tasks during the experiment.
rows.notes: Within-subject design where participants completed tasks both with and without AI. Sample size n=1499 is found in Table 2 (Appendix A). The SD can be calculated from the standardized effect: the paper reports 0.14 standard deviations for a 0.2-point difference, which implies SD ≈ 0.2/0.14 ≈ 1.43. This is consistent with the Root MSE = 1.3865 reported in Table 4 for the quality outcome. Using SD = 1.4 as a conservative estimate based on these values.

rows.title: Experimental Evidence on the Productivity Effects of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rows.task_description: we recruit 444 experienced, college-educated professionals and assign each to complete two occupation-specific, incentivized writing tasks. The occupations we draw on are marketers, grant writers, consultants, data analysts, human resource professionals, and managers. The tasks, which include writin
rows.notes: Level E extraction: standardized_effect_reported explicitly states '0.45 SD increase', which is treated as Cohen's d. This is a between-subjects randomized trial with n=222 per arm. The paper reports p=0.000 exactly.

rows.title: AI-prompted peer feedback: Can an AI feedback coach enhance peer feedback quality?
rows.onet_label: Interpreting the Meaning of Information for Others
rows.task_description: Participants provided peers with feedback on their assignments.
rows.notes: The verbatim quote states 'their feedback was significantly less criteria-based' for the AI group, which suggests the AI group had LOWER criteria-based feedback. However, the positive d=0.203 and positive t=2.014 indicate the AI group had HIGHER values. The language may be describing the control group. Given the statistical direction (positive t and d), I'm treating larger values as better (more criteria-based feedback). The comparison is between iteration 1 intervention (AI) vs iteration 1 control (no AI).

rows.title: The Uneven Impact of Generative AI on Entrepreneurial Performance: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Kenya
rows.task_description: Entrepreneurs could ask follow-up questions, explore alternatives, and learn more before deciding whether to implement advice.
rows.notes: The primary outcome is a standardized business performance index aggregating weekly and monthly profits and revenues. Figure 3 and Table 1 report the average treatment effect as β = 0.04 standard deviations (p = 0.34, 95% CI = [-0.05, 0.15]). The paper states 'we are unable to reject the null hypothesis of no average treatment effect on firm revenues and profits' (page 3). The study used stratified randomization by quartiles of pre-treatment business performance and by gender, with 640 total participants (320 per arm). The effect size of 0.04 s.d. is interpreted as a standardized effect (Cohen's d). The paper reports heterogeneous effects by baseline performance: low performers had β = -0.08 s.d. (p = 0.01), high performers had β = 0.16 s.d. (p = 0.07), with a heterogeneous treatment effect of β = 0.23 s.d. (p = 0.01) between the two groups.

rows.title: Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Employee's Productivity in Technological Organizations in Delta State, Nigeria
rows.task_description: questionnaires shared to over 150 employees of technological firms in Delta state
rows.notes: The study distributed 156 questionnaires and received 108 responses (69% response rate). The hypothesis testing tables show t-tests for questions 1-4 related to AI's effect on employee productivity. Question 1 directly tests 'Artificial Intelligence (AI) has a significant effect on employee productivity' with mean difference = 21.60000, t = 2.097, df = 4, p = .104 (two-tailed). The summary table shows 48 SA + 45 A = 93 positive responses vs 10 D + 0 SD = 10 negative for question 1. The paper states 'out of 156 copies of questionnaires that were distributed, 108 were returned' and the hypothesis test uses these data. The t-test appears to be conducted on aggregated Likert-scale responses. The df=4 is unusually low given n=108 respondents, suggesting the analysis may have been conducted on grouped/summarized data rather than individual responses. The means (21.6 vs 22.0) are extracted from the one-sample t-test tables comparing questions about AI presence. The p-value is reported as .104, which is not statistically significant at conventional levels. Sample sizes are inferred from the total N=108 split between conditions, though the exact allocation is not explicitly stated; assuming equal randomization gives n=54 per arm.

rows.title: The Invisible Hand of Gen-AI: Can AI-Enhanced Study Groups Improve Learning Outcomes?
rows.task_description: Students worked individually or in groups, with or without AI support.
rows.notes: Baseline means and SDs are available for all arms from the first exam. The treatment effects are reported as difference-in-differences estimates comparing post-treatment exams to baseline. The p-value for group_discussion_ai vs control on the third exam is reported as 'p < .01' (inequality only, not exact value). Using baseline descriptive statistics provides the cleanest comparison of the two arms.

rows.title: Generative AI's Impact on Graduate Student Writing Productivity and Quality
rows.task_description: A sequence of professional memo writing exercises was designed to consistently involve the same five core professional research and writing sub-tasks of summarization of a news article, web research to find survey results, analysis and synthesis of different survey results, policy development, and c
rows.notes: Within-subjects design with repeated measures ANOVA. Sample size n=26 inferred from df2=25 (n-1). The F-statistic is from a repeated-measures design. Mean difference = 94.1 - 88.3 = 5.8. The paper reports η²=.16 (eta-squared), not Cohen's d or Hedges' g. SD of difference scores not reported, but means, SDs, and F-statistic are available for paired-design Hedges' g calculation.

rows.title: Using Artificial Intelligence for Fraud Risk Assessment: Evidence from Novice and Experienced Auditors
rows.task_description: Participants review background information about the audit client, information about the industry and macroeconomic trends, details about sales and receivables, and preliminary analytical procedures before being asked to identify and list any fraud risk factors for the engagement.
rows.notes: Complete Level A data extracted from Table 4 Panel A (page 47). Sample sizes were found in the table cells: n=38 for No AI Assistance and n=41 for Has Access to AI Assistance. Standard deviations were also provided in the table: sd=2.58 for control and sd=2.84 for AI condition. The p-value is reported as an inequality (p-value= <0.01) rather than an exact value, so reported_p_value is set to null. The outcome is Number of Highly Relevant Risks, where higher values indicate better fraud risk identification performance.

rows.title: Large Language Model in Creative Work: The Role of Collaboration Modality and User Expertise
rows.task_description: In the experiment, participants were asked to complete an ad copywriting task. Specifically, they were tasked with creating advertisements for an iPhone protective case.
rows.notes: Summary statistics extracted from Table 1 on page 13. The table provides mean ad clicks and standard deviations for each condition. No-AI (Control): Mean=16.2, SD=6.6, N=117. Sounding board: Mean=16.4, SD=6.2, N=122. Ghostwriter: Mean=13.0, SD=5.0, N=116. The prior extraction missed these descriptive statistics which are clearly presented in the summary table. The regression models in Table A2 show the sounding board effect is not significant overall (β=0.140, p>0.1 in Model 4) but is significant for non-experts specifically (β=2.296, p<0.05 in Model 5). The means and SDs allow computation of Hedges' g using Level A data.

rows.title: Lawyering in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
rows.task_description: We selected the four assigned tasks—drafting a complaint, a contract, a section of an employee handbook, and a client memo—because they typify the type of work performed by young attorneys.
rows.notes: Within-subjects crossover design with n=30 participants completing tasks both with and without GPT-4. Means and SDs are provided for each condition, along with the mean difference (0.17) and its 95% CI. The SD of the paired difference scores is not explicitly reported, so Python will need to use the correlation-based approach or approximate from the CI width. The reported SDs (0.59 and 0.50) are the marginal SDs for each condition, not the SD of differences.

rows.title: Lawyering in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
rows.task_description: We selected the four assigned tasks—drafting a complaint, a contract, a section of an employee handbook, and a client memo—because they typify the type of work performed by young attorneys.
rows.notes: Level A extraction: means and SDs directly reported. Group A (n=30) used GPT-4 for complaint drafting; Group B (n=29) did not use AI for complaint drafting. The reported difference is 0.17 grade points with 95% CI (-0.03, 0.37).

rows.title: The Effects of Generative AI on High-Skilled Work: Evidence from Three Field Experiments with Software Developers
rows.task_description: The developers work on building a wide range of software within Microsoft, with tasks that include engineering, designing, and testing software products and services.
rows.notes: Table 2 (Balance Table) provides pre-treatment means and SDs for Build Success Rate: Control mean=0.72 (SD=0.30), Treatment mean=0.75 (SD=0.29), with p-value=0.33 for the difference test. Sample sizes are derived from the Microsoft experiment description: 1,746 developers total, with 50.4% randomized to treatment, yielding approximately 880 treatment and 866 control. The paper states 'We find no negative effect on the build success rate' and Table 3 shows a W-IV estimate of -5.53% (SE: 3.64) which is not statistically significant. The pre-treatment balance statistics provide the cleanest comparison for computing Hedges' g, as they represent baseline measurements before any treatment effect. The treatment effect estimate in Table 3 is expressed as a percentage change from baseline, not as raw means, so we use the pre-treatment values.

rows.title: No Great Equalizer: Experimental Evidence on Productivity Effects of Generative AI Use in the UK Labor Market
rows.task_description: Study participants performed three tasks of varying complexity (see Table 1) while being instructed to use ChatGPT (treatment group) or not use ChatGPT (control group).
rows.notes: Mean for AI arm calculated as 3.1 + 1.26 = 4.36 from reported effect. Standardized effect explicitly reported as '0.92 SD' which we treat as Cohen's d. P-value reported as inequality 'p < 0.001' so exact value set to null. Regression coefficient confirms the 1.262 point increase with p < 0.001.

rows.title: When GenAI increases inequality: evidence from a university debating competition
rows.task_description: The challenge consisted in three to four rounds of short one-on-one debates on public policy topics over a three-hour session.
rows.notes: The paper reports a standardized effect of 0.15 standard deviations for the overall treatment effect. The effect is described as not statistically significant (p-value not exactly reported, only stated as 'not statistically different from zero'). Multiple subgroup analyses are reported showing heterogeneous effects (e.g., 0.8 SD for high-ability students, 0.47 SD for top 50% baseline, 0.38 SD for strong evidence position), but the primary overall effect is 0.15 SD.

rows.title: AI-Powered Lawyering: AI Reasoning Models, Retrieval Augmented Generation, and the Future of Legal Practice
rows.task_description: We asked each participant to complete six realistic legal tasks developed in collaboration with practicing lawyers.
rows.notes: This is a between-subjects design with three arms. The paper reports standardized effects as '0.30 to 0.35 standard deviations' for o1_preview vs control. I extracted the midpoint (0.325) as Cohen's d. The regression coefficients show β = 0.52*** (SE 0.09) without participant FEs and β = 0.53*** (SE 0.09) with participant FEs, but without raw means and SDs, we rely on the reported standardized effect. The p-value is indicated as highly significant (***) but no exact value is provided.

rows.title: Generative AI Can Improve Performance and Engagement without Harming Learning
rows.task_description: Students work on curriculum-aligned math problems delivered through the tablet interface.
rows.notes: Table 2, column (3) reports the main effect on correctness rate: coefficient = 0.0264, SE = 0.0011, p < 0.01 (indicated by ***). The t-statistic can be computed as 0.0264 / 0.0011 = 24.0. The degrees of freedom are N - k where N = 2,114,829 observations (student-day level) and k is the number of parameters (student FE + date FE + controls). With large N and fixed effects, df ≈ N - number of students - number of dates. Given the massive sample size, df is effectively N - 18,904 students - ~365 dates ≈ 2,095,560, but conservatively using N-2 = 2,114,827 for the df. The outcome is measured in percentage points (0.0264 = 2.64 percentage points increase in correctness rate). The paper reports p < 0.01 but not an exact p-value. The effect is from a difference-in-differences regression with student and date fixed effects, comparing treated vs control students post-AI rollout. This is an observational study with quasi-random assignment based on device availability, not a true RCT, but the parallel trends assumption is supported by Figure 4 and the paper's robustness checks.
